quoteFirst: On the 4th, while we were waiting for a wind, we amused ourselves by fishing, and gathering shells and seeds of various kinds; and early in the morning of the 5th, we cast off the hawser, hove short on the bower, and carried the kedge-anchor out, in order to warp the ship out of the cove, which having done,
quoteTime: about two o’clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: , we hove up the anchor and got under sail; but the wind soon failing, we were obliged to come to an anchor again a little above Motuara.
title: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World. Vol. I. Being the First of the First Voyage.
author: Cook, James, 1728-1779; Banks, Joseph, 1743-1820 [Contributor]; Hawkesworth, John, 1715?-1773 [Editor]

quoteTime: about two o'clock in the afternoon
title: African Nature Notes and Reminiscences

quoteTime: About two o'clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: a bombardment of the town began; the fire was concentrated near the Tifonty Mill and grew heavier every minute.

quoteLast: , but owing to the speed of the other vessels we were last in.

quoteFirst: On Friday, the 30th December,     I came into the shop
quoteLast: It was a very quiet     day.
title: The Gun Alley Tragedy: Record of the Trial of Colin Campbell Ross

quoteFirst: We were, however, not without hopes of getting round the western point of the reef and into the bay, till
quoteTime: about two o’clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: , when we came before an opening or break in the reef, through which I hoped to get with the ships.
title: The Three Voyages of Captain Cook Round the World, Vol. III. Being the First of the Second Voyage
author: Cook, James, 1728-1779; Forster, Georg, 1754-1794

quoteLast: , for want of something better to do, I climbed the high mountain in front of our camp.

quoteFirst: Our air plane first reported that a certain fort, which stood about a mile in advance of the enemy's left flank, was strongly held; but we seem to have shelled them out of that pretty easily, for
quoteLast: it reported again that the enemy had left his trenches on the Canal.
title:

author: Palmer, Robert Stafford Arthur, 1888-1916

quoteTime: About two o’clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: of November 4th, large clouds of extraordinary blackness enveloped the mountains of the Brigantine and Tataraqual, extending gradually to the zenith.
title: Graham's Magazine, Vol. XL, No. 4, April 1852
author: Various; Graham, George R., 1813-1894 [Editor]
